title: Lesson 421
transcript: Code signing. So we just talked about digital signatures. Code signing is simply the use of digital signatures to verify some code, to make sure you know who some code came from, and to make sure that that code wasn't changed in transit. The perfect example I always like to think about is, let's say that my iPhone has told me that there is a software update available from Apple. If I'm about to install a software update on my iPhone, I'd really like to make sure that that update actually came from Apple and that that software update wasn't modified in transit. to make, you know, someone included some backdoor or something like that or vulnerability in the software. So how could I check that? How could I make sure that this code actually came from Apple and no one modified in transit? And the answer is code signing. And all code signing is, is basically creating a digital signature for some code. So here's exactly how your iPhone does it, or your Android phone does exactly this. You've actually probably noticed your phone doing this. If your phone says, hey, there's an update available, and you say, install. or download and install, or whatever the button says. You may have noticed it takes a few minutes to download, and then once it's downloaded, it doesn't install right away. For a few minutes, it actually usually takes a little while, it says, Verifying. If you ever wondered what exactly is your phone doing when it's verifying, the answer is it's looking at the digital signature that's attached to the software update. So here's how this works. When Apple creates a new software update, they will generate a digital signature for that software update. Remember how you create a digital signature is the sender hashes the data that they want to send. So they would hash the code, the software update. The sender would then encrypt that hash value with the sender's private key. and that would create a digital signature. So Apple would hash their software update and then they would encrypt that hash value with their Apple's private key. And so the software update you download comes with a digital signature attached to it. And what your phone's doing when it's verifying The first thing your phone is doing is it's decrypting the digital signature attached to the software update. And if you have an iPhone, it's gonna be decrypting that digital signature with Apple's public key now if the digital signature decrypts with Apple's public key who is the only organization in the world that should have created that digital signature Apple with their private key. So this first step here, right, the fact that the digital signature decrypts with Apple's public key, that proves authenticity. You now at least know, OK, this digital signature has to have come from Apple because they're the only ones that could have created it with their private key. Okay, now, once your phone has decrypted the digital signature, contained within that digital signature is gonna be the hash value that Apple created. And now the final step that your phone is gonna do is your phone is going to hash the software update that it received, generating a new hash value. And your phone is then gonna compare the hash value that it just generated from the hash value that was contained within Apple's digital signature. And if those hash values match, that proves integrity, it proves that your phone has received exactly the same software that Apple sent. So this is code signing. It's simply the use of digital signatures in signing code. So you can verify the integrity and the authenticity of some code. So that's the whole idea of code signing. It's just the process of creating a digital signature, right? You take some code, you hash that code, which of course produces a fixed length message digest, a hash value. And then you encrypt the sender, in this case, Apple. Apple would encrypt that hash value with Apple's, the sender's, private key. And that's created a digital signature, which you attach to the code. So Apple does this. Android does this. Microsoft does this. So it's used all the time in software updates. There's other uses for this as well. Let's say you're a company with a bunch of software developers and you've got software developers uploading code to a central repository, like, say, a GitHub repository. If some new code is being added to the repository, would you like to make sure that code actually came from one of your developers and that code wasn't modified in transit? You can do that with digital signatures. You can sign the code. So your developer, before they submit their code to the repository, your developer can, on their system, and their development, their IDE, their development environment can do this for them. When they go to upload some code, the development environment will generate a new digital signature for that code that's being uploaded. And then that way, say, GitHub can say, OK, yeah, did this code actually come from a known developer, one of your company's developers? And was this code modified in transit? Does it have integrity? So this is the idea of code signing. It's simply the application of digital signatures to signing code to verify the integrity and authenticity of code. So that's code signing. And here's the definition.
